observations.recommendation:
1.20 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Create an Inventory Committee to spearhead the conduct of annual
physical count of all PPE, promptly prepare the Report on the
Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE), and submit the report to the
Audit Team no later than January 31st of each year;
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal GSO to prepare
and maintain the PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards,
respectively. Also, both offices to reconcile their PPE records
regularly to ensure the accuracy and completeness of reports
supporting the PPE account balances in the financial statements;
and
c. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to expedite the reconciliation
and to take up the necessary adjustments to correct the balance of
Other Property, Plant, and Equipment (OPPE) account.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive reiterate its directive to the Inventory Committee to conduct the one-time Annual physical cleansing of the Municipality’s PPE and to submit the RPCPPE, among others, not later than January 31 of each year, for the prompt verification of the accuracy, completeness, and existence of PPE accounts.
We also recommended that the General Services Officer and Municipal Accountant to facilitate the updating of their respective Property Cards and PPE Ledger Cards to expedite the reconciliation of PPE records.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant review and identify the individual PPE accounts that need rectification, and to take up the necessary accounting adjustments/corrections thereto.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Reclassify or transfer the PPEs to Investment Property account; and
b. Ensure that all disclosures pertaining to Investment Property are incorporated in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct:
a. The Municipal Treasurer to:
   i. Ensure accuracy and completeness in the preparation of the Report of Checks Issued in accordance with Sections 43 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs; and
   ii. Prepare and provide a copy of the list of unreleased checks to the Municipal Accountant at the end of the year.
b. The Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments for unreleased checks at year-end to correct the Cash in Bank and corresponding Payable accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Review further the previous year’s charges against the Special Trust Fund and make the appropriate accounting adjustments; and
b. Draw the necessary journal entries to recognize the transfer of the completed project to the General Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the variance between the Statement of Financial Position and the Notes to the Financial Statements, and effect the necessary accounting adjustments.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the corresponding subsidiary ledgers and reconcile the balances thereof with their respective control accounts at the end of every month. The said ledgers should then be submitted not later than the fourteenth day of February of every year.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, Municipal Engineer/BAC Chairperson, MPDC, Municipal Budget Officer, and other concerned officials:
a. Consider reducing the total number of programs/projects/activities (PPAs) to be implemented for the year as a strategy to manage the utilization of the 20% Development Fund effectively;
b. Together with the Municipal Development Council, reassess the implementability and necessity of the unrendered and generic-termed PPAs, and reallocate the available balances to finance other much-needed development PPAs that are well-planned and procurement-ready; and
c. Make use of the SAAOB for effective monitoring of the projects.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MDRRMO Head, the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, and the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate the 30% Quick Response Fund in lump-sum and present the same in the LDRRMFIP.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive refrain from conducting study tours and other similar activities not fully compliant with the pertinent provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 02.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Engineer/BAC Chairperson and other personnel in charge of receiving the post-bidding documents to:
a. Demand for a revised or new surety bond that is properly accomplished and valid until the issuance of Certificate of Final Acceptance; and
b. Scrutinize the completeness and validity of the required documents submitted by the contractor and ensure submission of valid performance security before the signing of contracts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to require the submission of Official Receipts or other forms of proofs of transaction for taxi, bus, and ferry fares. We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the creation of an internal policy on allowed claims on different modes of transportation, including personal vehicles, based on customary fares per destination or actual distance traveled.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the concerned employees to submit the approved Travel Order and refund the excess travel claims.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to record cash advance initially as receivable and then the appropriate expense account upon its full liquidation. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to verify the completeness of the supporting/liquidation documents of the donations to prevent suspension in audit.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer ensure prospectively that all accruals and obligations made at year end are actually incurred in the same year and supported with documentary requirements to that effect.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Facilitate full implementation of prior-year audit recommendations concerning accounting adjustments; and
b. In coordination with the Human Resources Management Officer, conduct a root cause analysis on the delayed submission of the financial statements and implementation of prior years’ audit recommendations, giving attention to the current manpower complement and division of work in the Municipal Accounting Office.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Compute the depreciation expense for CY 2021 for each noted PPE account and provide the necessary adjusting entries to correct the affected balances in the financial statements; and
b. Make sure that all depreciable assets are depreciated every year.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exhaust measures in reconciling the Municipality’s record with that of the Iloilo Provincial Government and in determining the causes of the discrepancies. Upon reconciliation, proper adjustments should be made, if warranted.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MDRRMO to prepare the LDRRMFIP in accordance with the pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and include the list of PPAs chargeable against the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years for transparency and efficient monitoring of funds.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to set up and maintain complete subsidiary records for each special account, and prepare accordingly the trial balance and financial statements for each special account in the General Fund at the end of each year.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Market Supervisor to strategize more effective measures to collect monthly rentals from market stallholders and impose strictly the penalties for late or non-payment thereof. We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive execute a contract of lease with all the stallholders to support the leasehold rights of both parties.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Assessor and to reconcile the Land account and maintain subsidiary ledger in order to ensure reliability of account as presented in the financial statements. We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned personnel to exert efforts and make the necessary research for the retrieval of documents supporting the untitled parcels of land.
We further recommended that the concerned Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the 2015 PEI amounting to ₱2,186,450.00.
We recommended and the Municipal Health Officer and the Medical Officer V agreed to refund the excess amount of Hazard Allowance totaling ₱68,469.00.
We recommended the refund of the excess amount of Productivity Enhancement Incentive for CY 2014 amounting to ₱3,609,000.00 paid to officials and personnel.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to reiterate its directive to the Inventory Committee to conduct the one-time cleansing of the Municipality’s PPE and cause the eventual physical count and reconciliation of the PPE balances in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006, and submit the RPCPPE, among others, not later than January 31 of each year, in order for the accuracy and existence of PPE accounts to be verified in a timely manner.

We also recommended and the GSO Department Manager and the Municipal Accountant agreed to facilitate the updating of their respective Property Cards and PPE Ledger Cards to expedite the reconciliation of PPE records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the necessary adjustments to Construction Materials Inventory and the appropriate PPE accounts, to which depreciation shall be computed and applied.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to adjust the entries made to reflect the increase in capitalization threshold and appropriately disclose in the financial statements the effects of such change. 

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to review and examine henceforth the rules and guidelines governing the implementation of accounting policies or changes therein prior to adopting such to ensure the financial statements’ accuracy and reliability.

We recommended and the Municipal Health Officer agreed to facilitate the submission of the quarterly summary/list of COVID-19 vaccines and other aids and donations received, and its supporting documents within 10 working days after the end of each quarter.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to disclose prospectively in-kind donations received in the Notes to the Financial Statements, and record the cost of the remaining balance of said donations in the books in the event the quarantine or pandemic ends.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to present prospectively in the Statement of Financial Performance the gross amounts of collections from RPT and SET and of penalties and discounts thereon.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to facilitate the transfer to the STF in the Trust Fund books and record the appropriate adjustments. 

We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure that the transfer to the STF in the Trust Fund books be made at year end for the unexpended balances to be incorporated in the ensuing year’s LDRRMFIP and be recognized properly in the Statement of Financial Performance.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Municipal Development Council and concerned offices to be specific in the identification of projects/programs/activities (PPAs) to be charged against the 20% Development Fund so that the PPAs included in the Annual Investment Plan and Annual Budget already contain or provide sufficient details and costing.

We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the immediate submission to the Office of the Auditor of a copy of the authorization from the Sanggunian to validate the subject transactions.

We further recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure that prior authorization is secured from the Sanggunian for the implementation of PPAs stated in generic terms and for the payment of contracts thereof, granting that such practice is inevitable or merited.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Municipal Assessor to secure independent appraisal report/s on the two lots from the Appraisal Committee of the Iloilo Provincial Government or from any of the authorized appraisers, and submit said report/s to the Office of the Auditor as soon as possible.

We recommended and the Municipality agreed to discontinue the practice of allocating funds for Donations, or any expense item that is discretionary in nature, under the Legislative Services. The Vice-Mayor and the SB Members should rather endorse the requests for financial assistance from their constituents to the Office of the Mayor for appropriate action.

We also recommended and the Municipality agreed that any unobligated balance of the Donations budget under the Legislative Services for CY 2023 be augmented to other deficient items of the OSB within the same expense class. 

We further recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Liga President to spearhead the Liga in:

Establishing parameters for its selection of deserving beneficiaries; and

Engaging in more revenue-generating or fund-raising activities for the augmentation of funds for the Liga’s future projects and activities, and not rely heavily on financial aids from the Municipality.
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure prospectively thorough review of the journal entries made by her staff to prevent the erroneous recording of transactions.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to record the adjusting entries to correct the overstatement of Due from LGUs and Accounts Payable.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to drop from the list of recipient-barangays those with pending liquidation of previous financial assistance so that checks will not be prepared in their favor.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require full liquidation of financial assistance granted to the barangays and other beneficiaries as soon as possible by sending them demand letters.

We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned personnel to provide additional and more specific conditions in the MOA regarding the periods of completion and submission of liquidation papers to the Municipality.

We further recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to oblige the concerned officers to review and verify the correctness of the information and conditions stated in the MOA before approving such.
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure henceforth that any unutilized amount of the LDRRMF be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the GF at the lapse of five years.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct all the concerned officials and employees to cause the immediate liquidation of their unliquidated cash advances. Otherwise, any amount due to the concerned employees, whose cash advances have been outstanding for more than two months or 60 days already, should be withheld.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to submit a written explanation on the noted deficiency and direct the concerned personnel to compel the supplier to return the required creditable tax from the transaction.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned personnel to submit the lacking documents and a written justification on the deficiencies noted regarding the travel of the municipal officials/personnel to the National Literacy Conference and Awards.

Hence, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the
Inventory and Disposal Committee to, in cooperation with all offices concerned, to prioritize the
implementation of the one-time cleansing of PPEs, including reconciliation of
accounting records versus physical count pursuant to the provisions and
guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We also recommended that the Committee continue disposing unserviceable
properties in accordance with the guidelines set forth in Supply and Property
Management under COA Circular No. 92-836 dated October 20, 1992.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record all subsequent financial
assistance from the national government under Trust Liability-DRRMF pursuant to
COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
prepare the correcting journal entries on the discrepancies noted for the month of
May and August 2021 totaling P4,008.00. Likewise, recognize prior year income and
receivables from 2021 billings, net of collections received during the year.
For ensuing years, recognize receivables and hospital income based on hospital
billings as earned rather than upon collection in order to present the correct annual
income and year-end receivable balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Chairman of the Bids and
Awards Committee to require the BAC Secretariat and TWG to properly conduct
post-qualification of bids such that no award shall be made to contractors with the
deficiencies/inconsistencies in their bids as noted in the audit.

Based on the forgoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the following:
a. the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO)
implement the programs and projects of the Municipality under 70 percent
Mitigation Fund within the intended time to avoid delay in the delivery of its
benefits to the constituents and include in the LDRRMFIP plans for the Trust
Liability-DRRMF.
b. the Municipal Budget Officer to revert savings for allotment to other
preparedness and mitigation activities and conduct periodic reconciliation
with the Municipal Accountant and the MDRRM Officer to monitor the sources
and utilization of 5 percent LDRRMF.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the
submission of PPMPs of all offices/departments during budget preparation to the
Municipal Budget Officer for thorough evaluation in terms of amount and
coherence with the proposed budget of the Municipal Government to ensure that
all expenses/procurement items appropriated with funds are incorporated in the
Annual Procurement Plan. We also recommend that the BAC be required to
consolidate the PPMPs into an indicative Annual Procurement Plan for goods and
infrastructure projects simultaneous with the budget preparation in strict
adherence to the provisions of 2016 IRR of R.A. No. 9184 relative to budget
linkage, procurement planning and activities.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer to:
a. assess and recommend assistance based on the actual costs of the expenses
to be defrayed by the client but within any limitation to be set; and
b. review the existing ordinance of the Municipality in the grant of financial
assistance, particularly those provisions which were noted in audit and
propose to the Municipal Mayor and Sanggunian Bayan the necessary
amendments/adjustments.

Accordingly, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to
immediately submit the lacking documents listed in the Annexes for further
evaluation of the transactions.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to
strictly comply with the prescribed documentary requirements before payment is
effected.

Hence, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC Chairman and
the Municipal Accountant to submit the noted lacking procurement
documentation as well as for the distribution of welfare goods.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all concerned Department
Heads/employees to submit detailed action plans, issues and concerns
encountered relative to the implementation, and the specific time frame within
which they intend to comply on the CY 2020 AAR findings and recommendations,
including that of prior years, to be incorporated in the AAPSI, and to submit the
AAPSI to the Audit Team. Likewise, assign a focal person who will prepare the
AAPSI and monitor the compliance of each concerned Department Head/employee
regarding the submission of detailed action plans.

Subsequently, we recommended that the Management follow strictly the above
provisions of the GAA and COA Memorandum No. 2014-002 on the submission of
the AAPSI within 60 days from the receipt of the COA Annual Audit report to give
enough time for the Auditor to review, monitor and evaluate the action taken by
the management on the audit recommendations.

In view of the regulation on the one-time cleansing of PPE, we recommended that the
Municipal Mayor require the inventory and Disposal Committee to dedicate a period of
the year, as guided by a Physical Inventory Plan, on which complete and thorough
physical count of the PPEs will be conducted in accordance to the said Circular. After
which, require the immediate reconciliation of count results with the accounting records
to facilitate the review of the noted variances, effect the necessary adjustments,
provision of necessary disclosures in the notes to financial statements, and the
necessary disposition for non-existing/missing items.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. record income in the Trust Fund books upon utilization of funds received from national
agencies or any other sources, whether private or government, that are considered as
non-exchange transactions; and
b. prepare and submit for succeeding years the Statement of Financial Performance for
the Trust Fund along with the other financial statements of the Municipality.

Alike in previous year, we recommended and the Management agreed that the
Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Business Permits and Licensing Officer and OIC-Market Supervisor to maintain a
detailed record on the rentals and goodwill payments of all stalls indicative of the
lease term and transfer of rights effected, if any, and submit to the Municipal
Accountant to facilitate recording of appropriate receivables and income; and
b. Municipal Accountant to support the receivables for unpaid rentals with subsidiary
ledgers, accrue receivables and income from any unpaid rentals as of every
reporting date and facilitate reconciliation with the reports of the Market Office.
Likewise, record receivables for the goodwill payments as they are already due and
demandable, and recognize the income and liability for the unearned portion.

Therefore, we recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor
require the Municipal Accountant to draw adjusting journal entries to reduce the RPT
and SET Receivables for the amount of shares of other local government units, record
receivables and income for the delinquencies and recognize as income of prior years
the remaining balance of the liability accounts under the Deferred RPT and SET Income
under the Prior Period Adjustment account.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to
accordingly to recognize income in the Trust Fund books for every subsequent
utilization of Trust Liabilities-DRRM funds.

To avoid duplication of assistance, we recommended and the Management agreed
that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the Municipal Social Welfare and Development
Office (MSWDO):
a. in coordination with the barangay officials, to continuously work hand-in-hand
in maintaining/updating profile of families under its jurisdiction to properly and
readily identify the eligible beneficiaries of various programs/projects which may be
initiated either by government or private sector;
b. to continue sending demand letters in coordination with the barangay officials to
those SAP beneficiaries who received duplicate assistance to exact payment;
c. to inform the Social Security System of the individuals reported as SSS SAP
beneficiary but actually received no benefit; and
d. to coordinate with the OIC-Regional Director of DSWD Regional Office IV-A and
the Social Security System (SSS) for the clarification of actions to be taken on the
SAP beneficiaries with duplication of assistance.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant
agreed to recognize as assets the noted unrecorded vehicles and prospectively,
record in the Trust Fund all alike transactions arising from utilization of trust
receipts. We also recommended and the General Services Officer agreed to
verify/trace the user of the noted vehicles and accordingly issue PAR.

Therefore, we recommended and the Management agreed that procurement
documents and contracts are in order before accepting deliveries, and that the BAC
ensure that brands/specifications are indicated in the canvass forms to establish
difference of items for fair comparison of prices.

Therefore, we recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor
require the MDRRMO and Municipal Treasurer to submit support of disbursements
totaling ₱45,736.00 from the cash donations received and the Municipal
Accountant, on such basis, to record the expenses incurred. We also
recommended and the Municipal Treasurer and MDRRMO agreed to account for the
disbursements of the remaining ₱490,977.00 and submit the same to the Municipal
Accountant for proper recording. Otherwise, produce said amount and accordingly
deposit to the bank account of the Municipality.

We also recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer
and Municipal Accountant to ensure that all cash collections of the Municipality
from all sorts of transactions be properly acknowledged by official receipts and
recorded in the daily report of collections and deposits as well as in the books of
accounts.

In view of the foregoing instances, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor will
require the Bids and Awards Committee to justify the purchase of digital duplicator
and other IT equipment above the suggested retail price and submit a copy of proof
of dealership of the suppliers.

We also recommended and the Management agreed to require the Bids and Awards
Committee to properly conduct post-qualification of bidders, require certificates of
being authorized resellers/dealers to ensure that the prices availed are the lowest
possible and to ensure that the quoting suppliers indicate the brand and identical
specification of the items being offered to facilitate evaluation of the Committee of
the reasonableness in the price differences.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and BPLO to verify, assess and validate the reported under collection and consequently demand the collection/payment thereof or recoup through adjustment of fees to be collected in the ensuing year. Also require business owners to submit audited financial statements and/or sworn declarations as basis for assessment.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer and BPLO to require audited statements or sworn declaration of gross sales or receipts as support in the assessment of fees was agreed to be implemented for 2020. No collection was made in 2019 for the noted amount.
observations.reason: No collection was made in 2019. Collection to be made upon renewal of permits for CY 2020.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the created Inventory Committee agreed to focus on the
completion of the physical count using the prescribed format of the Report on
Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and immediately
reconcile inventory results with accounting records.

We also recommended and the Management agreed to require the General Service
Officer the disposal of unserviceable items to declutter the accounting books of non-
value items.

Further, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to, upon
reconciliation, verify the unrecorded PPE and inexistent assets, particularly with
regard to their ownership and existence, respectively, prior to their recording in and
dropping from the accounting books and disclose in the Notes to the Financial
Statements the results of reconciliation and Inventory Report as well as the amount of
PPE items which had no available documents.

Alike in previous years, we recommended and the Management agreed to require the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to provide the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of
each year with a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the
amount due and collectible for the year as basis for the setting-up of
receivable. Likewise, reconcile taxpayer balances as of year-end with that
recorded in the books to facilitate the necessary adjustments; and
b. Municipal Accountant to utilize the said list in maintaining subsidiary
ledgers/schedule to facilitate reconciliation with the records of the Municipal
Treasurer at the end of each year.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require all municipal officials and
employees to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period pursuant to
COA Circular 97-002 and Executive Order 77.

In view thereof, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to withhold
those taxes arising from Accounts Payable still outstanding as of date and apply this
practice prospectively.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the:
a. Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and GSO with the
complete description and cost segregation of road components for all road
projects;
b. GSO to prepare and maintain road networks property cards and Report on Local
Road Network based on the report submitted by the Municipal Engineer;
c. Inventory Committee to conduct inventory count of all the Municipality’s road
network systems and accordingly submit a Report on the Physical Count of the
Road Network System as of the end of the year; and
d. Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the prescribed property ledger
cards for Local Road Networks and disclose all the required information for the
said property in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Budget
Officer to appropriate in the budget of the LEEs funds for the return of subsidies
received including those used for Personal Services. Further, strictly comply with the
prohibition of appropriating subsidies from General Fund for PS costs of LEEs.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to set-up and maintain
Special Accounts in the General Fund for the Municipality’s economic enterprises
and recognize as receivables of the General Fund proper all subsidies or transfers
made to augment the LEEs’ budget.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Bids and Awards
Committee, its Secretariat and TWG to:
a. Undertake all activities relative to procurement of goods acquired other than thru
bidding, from the posting of request, canvass, selection up to recommending the
award to the lowest responsive quotation;
b. Update the Municipality’s PhilGEPs profile and utilize the online platform up to the
shortlisting and award of bid notices.
We also recommended and the Management agreed to formally institute a Committee
on Canvass and establish centralized procurement under the General Services Office.

We recommended and the Management agreed to reconsider the declared LEEs and
require the Municipal Budget Officer to monitor the PS costs considering that PS of
said LEEs remain subject to the limitation set in RA 7160.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the:
a. OIC-Business Permit and Licensing Officer (BPLO) under the Municipal Treasury
Office to assess the permit fees and taxes for the 2019 operations of the noted
business establishments using alternative means of verification of sales and
employees and field inspection as basis and require payment, otherwise, order
cease of operations; and
b. the OIC-Market Administrator be directed to require the same for the 77 market
stalls and order cease of operation sans the permit.

Moving forward, we recommended and the OIC-BPLO agreed to strictly regulate
operations of business establishments such that no entity shall be allowed to operate
without permit and payment of applicable taxes.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the OIC-Market
Administrator and OIC-BPLO who were under the supervision of Municipal Treasurer to
strictly implement the prohibition on sub-lease as enunciated in the Revenue Code of
the Municipality.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the OIC-Market
Administrator to:
a. Submit to the Audit Team the updated and verified report on goodwill payments of all
the 1,040 stalls in coordination with the OIC-BPLO;
b. Collect the overdue goodwill money particularly those past the 5th year of their
lease term, check for and collect underpayments, require timely and sufficient
payments of goodwill money for all stalls and use the rates provided in the 2017
Bauan Market Code, as revised by the 2020 Market Code; and
c. Maintain individual records of goodwill payments of each stall to facilitate detailed
monitoring with reference to actual payments received by the Municipal Treasurer.

We also recommended and the OIC-BPLO agreed to furnish the Market
Administration copy of periodic summary of payments for their information and
monitoring. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to
recognize in the books of accounts the receivables for goodwill money and
adjustment of prior years’ income and maintain subsidiary ledgers for each
stallholder.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the:
a. OIC Market Administrator to demand settlement of the unpaid rentals including
penalties, otherwise revoke the lease contract and compel to vacate the stall; and
b. OIC Market Administrator to strictly implement the correct rates of market rental
fee and penalty, monitor payments of monthly rentals and timely issue the demand
letters to delinquent stallholders to exact payments and enforce Section 40 on the
revocation of lease for non-payment despite demand.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to recognize the
receivables of the noted unpaid rentals supported by individual subsidiary ledgers.
Prospectively, we recommended to accrue the monthly receivables and income from
rentals and establish individual subsidiary ledgers for each stallholder and reconcile
unpaid balances with the reports of the Market Administration as of any reporting
period.

Finally, we recommended and the Sangguniang Bayan agreed to make clarification/
amendments on the inconsistencies on the rates of market rental fee and penalty
provided in Section 12 of Revised Market Code of 2020 and Section 5A.01 of Article A
of Chapter 5 of Vol. II of Revised Revenue Code of Bauan 2020.

We recommended and the Management agreed to initiate the creation the Market
Committee for the strict implementation of the prescribed process and manner of
filing application and awarding stall rights.

We also recommended and the Management agreed that the OIC-Market
Administrator be required to:
a. Conduct inventory of contracts/documents and inspect each stall to establish the
legitimate stallholders and accordingly update records copy furnished the
Municipal Treasurer and Accountant and the Audit Team; and
b. Execute with the stallholders lease contracts aligned with the Market Code,
particularly with the ratified transferees from the original stallholders and propose
amendments to the provisions of the existing lease contracts to make it consistent
with the Code.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the Human Resource
Management Officer, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to control the
accommodation given to financing institutions and ensure that minimum monthly
take-home pay of all officials and employees shall not be lower than Five Thousand
Pesos (₱5,000.00) pursuant to General Appropriations Act.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor thru its MENRO agreed to enjoin the
barangay officials in the practice 4 Rs (Reduce, Re-use, Recycle and Recovery) and
segregation of waste at source in the municipality through strict enforcement of the
“no segregation no collection” policy and put the barangay MRFs into full
functionality in their respective barangay.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider establishment of General Service Office with sufficient staff and adopt adequate measures and provide efficient and effective property and supply management in the Municipality We also recommended to create an Inventory Committee and require the following: a. conduct of physical count of all properties of the Municipality, preparation of the report and reconciliation of inventory results against the accounting records. Considering these findings on PPE as a perennial issue of the Municipality, we recommended that these procedures be done by account until all PPE accounts in the financial statements are completed; and b. locate and secure PPE yet. ₱63,328,860.53. If whereabouts cannot be determined after exerting all efforts, trace accountability and require the persons accountable to prepare the incident report indicating the reasons of missing properties and if found negligent he shall pay the missing properties at cost.
observations.managementAction: The General Service Office was established in 2019 and the General Service Officer was hired on August 16, 2019. Said office conducted actual inventory of selected PPE accounts utilizing additional manpower (job orders).
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. General Assessor, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to determine the nature and amount of acquisition of these lots, as basis of valuation; b. General Assessor to conduct sites inspection and prepare the reports on the actual usage of each parcels of land and existence of unlawful occupants, if any, and provide the said report to the Municipal Accountant; c. Municipal Accountant to recognize in the Municipality’s books of accounts the two hundred twenty-five (225) parcels of land using the market value indicated in the earliest applicable Tax Declarations under the name of the Municipality; and d. Municipal Budget Officer include the amounting ₱450,000.00 under the appropriation for the titling of land owned by the Municipal Government.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Assessor determined the details of acquisition and submitted information to the Municipal Accountant to ascertain the nature and amount of acquisition of these lots, as basis of valuation; General Assessor conducted sites inspection and prepared the schedule showing actual usage of each parcels of land and existence of unlawful occupants, if any, and provided the said report to the Municipal Accountant. The 193 lot parcels amounting to ₱97,551,098.34 are recognized in the books of accounts. The municipal appropriation of ₱450,000.00 was appropriated for the titling of land owned by the Municipality under the Office of the Mayor per the approved Annual Budget for CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider an Inventory Committee to conduct the inventory monthly basis per department and submit the consolidation and submit the reports of inventories and reconcile with the Accounting Office. Reclassification of the land is discussed in 2018 Annual Audit Report.
observations.managementAction: Inventory of ordinary and common office supplies were counted on a monthly basis per department and submitted to the General Services Department for consolidation and subsequently submitted to the Accounting Office. Reclassification of the land was effected as discussed in 2018 Annual Audit Report.
observations.reason: Inventory of ₱1,293,581.40 as of December 31, 2019 was not supported by a physical count.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Municipal Treasurer and the Business Permits and Licensing Officer to require the business owner to submit and attach the verified list of employees stamped received as basis of assessment of fees. Ensure that the Application Form for Business Permit is completely and properly accomplished before processing and issuing business permits. We also recommended the Municipal Mayor require collection of the remaining undercollection of ₱33,306.00 and demand collection/payment thereof.
observations.managementAction: The Business Permits and Licensing Officer has been upgraded in 2019 and the Business Licensing system was upgraded in later part of 2019 corresponding to the revisions on the Municipal Revenue Code and took effect on transactions/collections of 2020.
observations.reason: The balance of ₱546.00 was already collected on August 5, 2019 and still in the process of collecting ₱32,760.00 as of report. The occupational fees will be reported separately starting January 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Budget Committee to study and analyze the effects of the 2013 Revised Revenue Code on the assessment and collection of fees and propose the necessary revisions. Also require the Local Finance Committee to consider updating the assessed based on the recent transactions and to update the system accordingly.
observations.managementAction: The Business Licensing system was upgraded in later part of 2019 corresponding to the revisions on the Municipal Revenue Code and took effect on transactions/collections of 2020. The Local Finance Committee studied and proposed updates; updates adopted in the 2019 Revised Revenue Code of Bauan 2020.
observations.reason: Same outdated profile of establishments were used for assessment of fees for CY 2019; system upgrade implemented later in 2019 and took effect in 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (MDRRMC) and the Municipal Budget Officer to prepare the LDRRM Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) for the current year appropriation indicating the detailed PPAs for implementation and that the Municipal Budget Officer maintain separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligation for LDRRMF pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: Separate RAAO is maintained. The Municipal Management and MDRRMF Management sources clarified and verified with the Municipal Budget Office. The LDRRMFIP for 2019 has sufficient details as to the planned activities and projects. The CDs are clarified and funds utilized. The unexpended Mitigation Fund was transferred.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor stop hiring the services of legal consultant pursuant to par. 3(1), Section 481 of R.A. 7160 and COA Circular No. 98-002.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality terminated the services of legal consultant and stopped payment for legal services.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor discontinue the practice of granting cash advances to Department of Education employees and designate a permanent disbursing officer for the Special Education Fund to perform disbursing functions; adhere strictly with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the grant and utilization of cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Accomplishment reports were required to support the payment of daily wages. Employees were designated and the Special Disbursing Officer was tasked to perform disbursing functions out of Special Education Fund. Funds for MOOE were no longer supported by cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to undertake a thorough study and evaluation, to be spearheaded by the HRMO, on the actual workloads and manpower requirements for each department/offices to maximize available resources; require accomplishment report as basis for the payment of wages of the job order personnel.
observations.managementAction: Accomplishment reports were required to support the payment of daily wages.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the MENRO inform the Solid Waste Management Board if the former dumpsite is compatible for an eco-park in coordination with the Municipality Engineer, MPDC and Municipal Budget Officer to continue to work on the full rehabilitation of the closed dumpsite and eventual conversion to an eco-park.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality requested for the geohazard assessment of the former dumpsite by the DENR Mines and Geosciences Bureau (MGB) where it was determined that the site is not compatible for an eco-park. The Municipality is now considering to make it their central Materials Recovery Facility (MRF) and motorpool.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to practice 4 Rs (Reduce, Re-use, Recycle and Recovery) and segregation of waste at source in the municipality through strict enforcement of the 'no segregation no collection' policy and put the barangay MRFs into full functionality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor thru the MENRO agreed to enjoin and coordinate with barangay officials and MPDC. The Municipality conducted information drives, distributed tarpaulins and flyers and started IEC campaigns in markets and barangays. The Municipality also repaired dump trucks and enforced measures.
observations.reason: The barangays do not fully prioritize solid waste management; implementation of MRF functionality varies by barangay.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Office of the Senior Citizen Administration update the master list of senior citizens indicating the economic status of each and that replacements be prioritized according to age and economic status as prescribed by DSWD Administrative Order No. 15 s. 2010.
observations.managementAction: Delisted beneficiaries were implemented and replacements were prioritized according to DSWD priority rules. The Municipal Administration conducted quarterly validation and monitoring of beneficiaries.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to consolidate requests and proceed to public bidding if necessary and implement centralized procurement under the General Services Office.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality considers centralization of procurement and effort to consolidate purchases; the General Services Office is being defined and will act as central procurement.
observations.reason: Items are not yet fully carried as stock by the Municipality; centralization process still ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor instruct the Supply Unit and Bauan General Hospital (BGH) to coordinate and expedite the physical count; prepare records and reconcile with accounting; maintain stock and property cards; prepare report for unserviceable properties for disposal; and investigate missing or unlocated properties and trace accountability.
observations.managementAction: Supply Unit of the Municipality and BGH conducted actual inventory and provided reports. The Supply Unit maintained list of unserviceable properties as of December 31, 2018. The Supply Unit monitored transfers and accountabilities, and property accountabilities of unserviceable properties are being traced. Some disposal processes are pending due to lack of proper procedure and knowledge.
observations.reason: Priority is yet to be given to disposal of unserviceable properties and tracing of accountability remains undone.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor instruct the Local Finance Committee to closely monitor the utilization of funds, consider strategies to increase income, intensify collection and prioritize programs; limit transfer of unexpended balances; and urge management to consider adjustments to ensure cash liabilities covered.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality complied with Sections 309(b) of RA 7160 and PD 1445 by transferring the receipts to Trust Fund on March 20, 2018 under OR Nos. 21826558 and 21826559. The Municipal Accountant effect corrections through JEVs. The management identified that major component of receivables was from PHILHEALTH and OWWA and amounts were recognized.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure all accountable officers liquidate cash advances within thirty (30) days after return and require immediate liquidation.
observations.managementAction: Accomplishment reports were required and enforced; cash advances granted to DepEd employees were discontinued.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer reconcile RPT and SET receivable balances; Municipal Treasurer to provide certified list of taxpayers and amounts due to Municipal Accountant at the beginning of each year; and the Municipal Mayor and Sangguniang Bayan adopt measures to collect delinquent taxes.
observations.managementAction: Setting up of receivables was made using available records; reconciliation efforts ongoing. Municipal Treasurer provided certified lists and conducted tax campaigns and coordination with barangays. Municipal Mayor through Sangguniang Bayan adopted measures to collect and provide tax information drives.
observations.reason: No full reconciliation yet; process still ongoing though measures adopted for collection and tax campaign.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to: a. record supplies purchases to appropriate asset accounts upon acquisition, maintain subsidiary ledgers, record issuances based on Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI); b. Supply Officers and BGH to maintain stock cards; prepare and submit monthly RSMI; and prepare and submit Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI).
observations.managementAction: Supplies are already recorded to asset accounts; RSMI is being submitted monthly to Accounting Office. The Supply Unit maintained the list of unserviceable properties as of December 31, 2018. BGH maintains records and monitors inventory.
observations.reason: Some RPCI submissions and full reconciliation of subsidiary ledgers remain pending; hospital supplies monitoring still being improved.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to setup and maintain Special Accounts in the General Fund for the Municipality’s economic enterprises and the 20% Development Fund, and prepare separate subsidiary ledgers and financial statements for each enterprise.
observations.managementAction: Transactions relative to the economic enterprises were recognized; Special Accounts remained not fully maintained but the Municipal Accountant provided separate tracking of major transactions. Financial utilization and transactions of the economic enterprise are monitored.
observations.reason: Separate books of accounts for economic enterprises and development fund are still not fully maintained though transactions tracked.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to continuously coordinate with the Provincial Government Accountant to reconcile differences of balances as of year-end and identify causes of discrepancies and effect adjustments.
observations.managementAction: Reconciliation actions undertaken; for 2018 receivable of the Municipality from the Provincial Government amounting to ₱1,065,758.47 was reconciled. The Municipal Accountant submitted certification to the Provincial Accountant regarding certain balances.
observations.reason: Some discrepancies remain and reconciliation is ongoing; timing differences in remittances contributed to differences.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to request write-off of receivables with no certainty of collection, and to pursue collection efforts for valid claims and provide proof of actions undertaken.
observations.managementAction: No write-off requests were prepared for certain accounts; efforts have been initiated for selected receivables. Some actions were undertaken including collection drives but several receivables remain outstanding.
observations.reason: No comprehensive write-off requested; collection efforts ongoing but incomplete; some accounts require further documentation to proceed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to exert effort to verify all unexpended/dormant balances, retrieve and evaluate supporting documents, remit or revert to appropriate source agencies or the general fund those with no legal claims.
observations.managementAction: Some amounts such as ₱10,650,000.00 were liquidated; others were investigated. Municipal Accountant effected necessary adjustments through journal entries where applicable. Some items lacked supporting documents impeding settlement.
observations.reason: Absence of supporting documents impedes settlement; some amounts already liquidated while others pending verification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Officials to submit necessary documents to facilitate evaluation of propriety of disbursements and compliance to procurement rules, prepare ABCs in accordance with DPWH guidance and ensure posting of notices in PhilGEPS.
observations.managementAction: There were efforts to comply with procurement requirements; Approved Budget of Contract (ABC) for sampled projects corrected per DPWH Department Order guidance. Some purchase requests were held pending due to compliance issues with RA 9184.
observations.reason: Some ABCs and procurement documentation required further adjustments; a number of projects had pending purchase requests and compliance actions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended submission of amended contract for revised estimates and change of name of winning bidder, the Notice to Proceed, Contract Agreement, and documents to support revised estimates for evaluation by Technical Audit Specialist (TAS).
observations.managementAction: Relevant documents were submitted and referred to TAS for evaluation of revised estimates. Some deleted items were identified and documentation provided; amended contracts and supporting letters were submitted to the Auditor.
observations.reason: Some documents were provided and under evaluation by TAS; certain matters required further clarification and were not fully resolved at reporting.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor enjoin the Municipal Agriculturist to revive vermi-composting, encourage multi-purpose cooperatives to promote projects, and the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board to continue IEC and strengthen implementation.
observations.managementAction: Municipality conducted IEC, distributed flyers and tarpaulins, and engaged stakeholders. Plans for vermi-composting were studied though viability issues arose regarding proximity and beneficiaries.
observations.reason: Barangays do not give priority to solid waste management; vermi-composting considered not viable in proposed locations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor to evaluate transfers made to other agencies, substantiate transfers with proper documents, and effect adjustments in the books; ensure submitted Hospital inventory reports are in prescribed format.
observations.managementAction: Some parcels were recorded; the Supply Unit and BGH conducted inventories though the inventory committee was not fully created as of year-end. Some transfers were substantiated and adjustments were made.
observations.reason: Inventory committee not created by year-end; hospital inventory not fully in prescribed format; tracing missing property accountability still ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipality to enforce compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 and ensure LDRRMF expenditures are for proper purposes; retrieve supporting documents for procurement and ensure transparency.
observations.managementAction: Some amounts were liquidated and adjustments effected. The Municipality instituted better controls and monitoring of LDRRMF expenditures and requested necessary supporting documents.
observations.reason: Some expenditures remain to be fully supported and procurement documentation improved but previous deficiencies still required rectification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended compliance with RA 9184 procurement procedures, prepare ABCs as per DPWH Dept Order No. 22 Series of 2015, and promptly implement purchase of DRRM equipment.
observations.managementAction: Purchase requests were processed; ABC preparation improved per DPWH guidance. Some projects were postponed pending RA 9184 compliance; QRF was utilized and some projects implemented.
observations.reason: Some items were pending due to compliance with RA 9184 and documentation requirements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipality submit supporting documents including amended contracts, revised estimates, change of name of winning bidders for TAS evaluation and ensure all documents are on file.
observations.managementAction: Submitted relevant documents and referred to TAS; amended contracts and letters provided; evaluation ongoing for revised estimates.
observations.reason: Some matters under TAS evaluation; full resolution pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended revive vermi-composting, continue IEC, encourage barangays to implement MRFs and ensure measures are not costly to constituents, and strengthen internal controls over disbursements for waste management.
observations.managementAction: IEC campaigns conducted; tarpaulins and flyers distributed; meetings with market section presidents; Municipality to continue IEC and coordination with barangays. Consideration for vermi-composting studied.
observations.reason: Barangays' participation varied and implementation inconsistent; internal control improvements ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor seek prior approval from the Commission on Audit on the disposal of every duly owned real properties to ensure terms and conditions are to the advantage of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Pending ruling of the COA Central Office on the legality of the contract entered into by the Municipality of Bauan relative to this matter. Fraud Audit Team conducted a fraud audit.
observations.reason: We are not in the position to state the status of the recommendation since the audit was conducted by the Fraud Audit Team and a fraud audit report is pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure compliance with Section 18 of the Rules and Regulation on Supply and Property Management in the Local Government prescribed under COA Circular No. 92-386 and Section 380 of RA 7160 and ensure that all conditions are to the advantage of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Pending ruling of the COA Central Office on the legality of the contract entered into by the Municipality of Bauan relative to this matter. Fraud Audit Team has also reviewed.
observations.reason: We are not in the position to state the status of the recommendation since the audit was conducted by the Fraud Audit Team and no information yet as to its status.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor consider
establishment of the General Services Office with sufficient staff and adopt adequate
measures and provide efficient and effective property and supply management in the
Municipality pursuant to Section 444 of RA 7160.

We also recommended and they have agreed to create an Inventory Committee and
require the following:
- conduct of physical count of all properties of the Municipality, preparation
of the corresponding report and reconciliation of inventory results against
the accounting records. Considering these findings on PPE as a perennial
issue of the Municipality, we recommend that these procedures be done by
account until all PPE accounts in the financial statements are completed;
and
- locate and secure PPE totalling ₱63,328,860.53. If whereabouts cannot be
determined after exerting all efforts, trace accountability and require the
persons accountable to prepare the incident report indicating the reasons
of missing properties and if found negligent he shall pay the missing
properties at cost.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. General Services Officer- Designate, Municipal Assessor, Municipal
Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to determine the nature and amount of
acquisition of these lots, as basis of valuation;
b. General Services Officer- Designate and Municipal Assessor to conduct
sites inspection and prepare the reports on the actual usage of each
parcels of land and existence of unlawful occupants, if any, and provide the
said report to the Municipal Accountant;
c. Municipal Accountant to recognize in the Municipality’s books of accounts
the two hundred twenty-five (225) parcels of land using the market value
indicated in the earliest applicable Tax Declarations under the name of the
Municipality; and
d. Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Assessor include in the
appropriations the titling of land owned by the Municipal Government.

We recommended and the Management agreed to have an Inventory Committee to
conduct physical count of the supplies and materials as of year-end, prepare and
submit the report of inventories and reconcile with the Accounting records the
balances per inventory reports submitted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer and the
Business Permits and Licensing Officer to require the business owner to submit and
attach the verified list of employees stamped received by the Social Security System
or its equivalent and their addresses, together with the document evidencing the
payment of occupational fees pursuant to the 2013 Revised Revenue Code of the
Municipality and maintain the said records for audit purposes. Ensure that the
Application Form for Business Permit is completely and properly accomplished
before processing and issuing business permits.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and
BPLO to verify, assess and validate the reported remaining undercollection of
₱33,306.00 and consequently demand the collection/payment thereof. The absence of
documented action or information to counter the undercollection shall mean the
issuance of the corresponding Notice of Charge.

We also recommended proper recording of the occupational fees in the books of
accounts in compliance with PPSAS.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require the:
• Municipal Treasurer and Licensing Officer to initiate the system update and
ensure that permit fees are properly assessed based on the recent and verified
asset size/workforce of business establishments using the corresponding
rates as prescribed by the Municipal Revenue Code; and
• Local Finance Committee to study and analyze the effects of the 2013 Revised
Revenue Code on the assessment and collection of permit fees and propose to
the Sanggunian Bayan necessary revisions.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
• stop the practices of subjecting the newly started business entities in the local
business tax and automatic application of additional 10 per cent increase on
the previous year’s gross receipts as a basis in the computation of local
business tax pursuant to BLGF Memorandum Circular No. 01-001-2017.
- require the Municipal Treasurer and the Business Permits and Licensing
Officer to require the business owner to submit and attach the audited
financial statement, or Sworn Declaration of gross sales or receipts, or its
Income Tax Returns (ITR) pursuant to the 2013 Revised Revenue Code of the
Municipality and maintain the records for audit purposes. Ensure that the
Application Form for Business Permit are completely and properly
accomplished before processing and issuing business permits; and
• require the Municipal Treasurer and BPLO to verify, assess and validate the
reported under collection totaling ₱708,688.76 and consequently demand the
collection/payment thereof or recouped through adjustment of fees to be
collected in the ensuing year. The absence of documented action or
information to counter the under collection shall mean the issuance of the
corresponding Notice of Charge.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (MDRRMC)
and the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer to prepare
the final LDRRMFIP indicating the detailed PPAs for implementation
specifically identifying the current year appropriation of 70 per cent
Mitigation Fund-MOOE and Capital Outlay and shall contain under a
separate caption, the list of projects and activities to be charged against
the unexpended balance of LDRRMF of previous years transferred in the
Special Trust Fund in Trust Fund book before the preparation of Annual
Budget;
b. Municipal Budget Officer to maintain separate Registry of Appropriation,
Allotments, and Obligation for LDRRMF pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-
002;

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor stop hiring the services of legal
consultant of the Commission on Audit pursuant to par. 3(1), Section 481 of R.A. 7160
and COA Circular No. 98-002.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor discontinue the
practice of granting cash advances to Department of Education employees and
designate a permanent employee of the Municipality as Special Disbursing Officer to
perform disbursing functions out of Special Education Fund. Henceforth, adhere
strictly with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the grant and utilization of
cash advances.

We also recommended and the Management agreed to stop charging maintenance and
other operating expenses of private schools from the Special Education Fund,
instead they may be charged to the General Fund of the Municipality.

We recommended and the Management agreed to undertake a thorough study and
evaluation, to be spearheaded by the HRMO, on the actual workloads and manpower
requirements for each department/offices to maximize available resources; and
require accomplishment report as one of the bases for the payment of wages of the
job order personnel.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the MENRO inform the Solid
Waste Management Board if the necessary additional gas vents and in coordination
with the Offices of the Municipal Agriculture, Municipal Engineer, MPDC and
Municipal Budget Officer continue to work on the full rehabilitation of the closed
dumpsite and eventual conversion to an eco-park.

We recommended and the Management agreed to practice 4 Rs (Reduce, Re-use,
Recycle and Recovery) and segregation of waste at source in the municipality
through strict enforcement of the “no segregation no collection” policy in all
barangays. Also, it is recommended and the Management assured to monitor and put
the barangay MRFs into full functionality.

We recommended and the Management agreed to enforce stringent controls on
reporting of usage, breakdown and repairs of the trucks and imposing sanctions to
the violating collectors so as not to cause further damage of the trucks and sustain a
regimented system and accountability;
We also recommended the repair of the dumptrucks and the Management commented
that it is already a work in progress.

We recommended and the Management agreed with the help of the Office of the
Senior Citizens Administration to update the master list of senior citizens indicating
the economic status of each senior citizen and implement selection in order of
priority prescribed under DSWD Administrative Order No. 15 series of. Likewise,
conduct quarterly validation and monitoring of the beneficiaries to determine their
situation, ascertain their eligibility and entitlement and to ensure optimal utilization of
the stipend for the full implementation of Social Pension Program of the government.

We recommended and the Management agreed to ensure that requests for supplies
not available from the PS-DBM shall be consolidated per quarter and proceed to
public bidding if necessary, as provided by the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

The Audit Team recommended that in entering into Lease Contracts and disposal of government properties, the Municipal Mayor must seek prior approval from the Commission on Audit on the subsequent disposal of every duly owned real properties, to ensure that all the terms and conditions attached therewith are to the advantage of the Municipality, thereby protecting its full interest and welfare.
observations.managementAction: Pending ruling of the COA Central Office on the legality of the contract entered into by the Municipality of Bauan relative to this matter; the Audit Team recommended that in the meantime, the Municipal Mayor seek prior approval from the Commission on Audit on the subsequent disposal of every duly owned real properties, to ensure that all the terms and conditions attached therewith are to the advantage of the Municipality.
observations.reason: We are not in the position to state the status of the recommendation since the audit was conducted by the Fraud Audit Team. This observation has a fraud audit report already.

observations.recommendation: The Audit Team recommended that in entering into Lease Contracts and disposal of government properties, the Municipal Mayor must see to it that the provisions of Section 18 of the Rules and Regulation on Supply and Property Management in the Local Government prescribed under COA Circular No. 92-386 and Section 380 of the Republic Act 7160 are complied with and ensure that all terms and conditions attached therewith are to the advantage of the Municipality, thereby protecting its full interest and welfare.
observations.managementAction: Pending ruling of the COA Central Office on the legality of the contract entered into by the Municipality of Bauan relative to this matter; the Audit Team recommended that the Municipal Mayor seek prior approval from the Commission on Audit on the subsequent disposal of every duly owned real properties, to ensure that all the terms and conditions attached therewith are to the advantage of the Municipality.
observations.reason: We are not in the position to state the status of the recommendation since the audit was conducted by the Fraud Audit Team; no information yet as to its status.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
- instruct the Supply Unit of the Municipality and Bauan General Hospital (BGH)
for the completion of the physical count of all properties of the Municipality
(including BGH) by creating an inventory team, providing them attainable time
table to accomplish the inventory taking and report preparation;
- instruct the Supply Unit of the Municipality and BGH to continuously coordinate
with all the department/offices of the Municipality to expedite the completion of
the physical count;
- instruct the Supply Unit of the BGH to submit immediately the required RPCPPE
report to the Audit Team. Otherwise, enforcement of Sec. 7.1.2 of COA Circular
No. 2009-006 is enjoined;
- instruct the Office of the Municipal Accountant and Supply Unit to evaluate the
causes of discrepancies and complete the preparation of inventory records for all
PPE accounts to ascertain the accuracy of the PPE balances in the financial
statements. Likewise, observe proper maintenance of the Property Cards per
category of PPE and Real Property Ledger Cards for real properties of the
Municipality;
- instruct the Supply Unit of the Municipality and BGH to have an updated list of
those items for disposal, prepare all the necessary documents/reports and act on
the properties to be disposed within reasonable time;
- instruct the Supply Unit to substantiate transfer of properties with proper
documents directed by Sections 185 and 186 of Rules and Regulations on Supply
and Property Management in Local Governments prescribed by COA Circular 92-
386; and
- instruct the Supply Unit of the Municipality and BGH to investigate on the cause
of the missing or unlocated properties. Identify who are accountable by tracing
the recipients from the Memorandum Receipt (MR) or the Acknowledgement
Receipt for Equipment (ARE). The accountable officer should request for the
relief from property accountability if the loss was not due to his/her negligence
but if found that he did not perform due care as that of a good father, he/she
should be held liable for the replacement cost of such property or equipment.
Refer to Sections 151 and 152 of COA Circular 92-386 on the requirements for
application for relief, if applicable.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant, the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Budget Officer to
coordinate closely in the use of funds and prepare daily cash position report in
order to regularly monitor the cash balances before incurrence of
disbursements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the officials and
employees to strictly observe the provisions of Section 309(b) of Republic Act
(RA) No. 7160 and Section 84 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Local
Finance Committee to closely monitor the utilization of funds, consider different
strategies to increase the Municipality’s income and intensify collection, impose
additional cost-cutting measures, prioritize projects and programs, and limit the
incurrence of obligations to the extent of cash availability.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
• Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the
₱2,614.61 variance between the General Ledgers and the Cashbooks and
to exert extra effort to look into the status of the book reconciling items
totaling to ₱98,815.97;
• Municipal Accountant to effect in the books the necessary adjustments to
record all the reconciling items noted in this observation duly supported
with valid documents to reflect the correct cash balance in the financial
statements; and
• Municipal Treasurer to have a separate Cashbook – Cash in Bank by bank
account and by fund to facilitate verification and monitoring
thereof of individual cash balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to:
• ensure that all cash advances are liquidated within the prescribed
period and require the accountable officers of the immediate and
timely liquidation of cash advances; and
• ensure that the cash advances granted to officers and employees do
not exceed their maximum cash accountability.

We recommended that:
•••• the Municipal Treasurer provide the Municipal Accountant at the
beginning of each year with a duly certified list showing the name of
taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year as basis for
the setting-up of receivable; Likewise, submit to the Sangguniang
Bayan certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which
remained uncollected or unpaid for at least one (1) year and a
statement of the reason or reasons for such non-collection or non-
payment on or before December thirty-first (31st) of the year
immediately succeeding the year in which the delinquencies were
incurred, with a request for assistance in the enforcement of the
remedies for collection;
•••• the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer reconcile
their records to present an accurate and reliable RPT and SET
Receivables in the financial statements; and
•••• the Municipal Mayor, as well as the Sangguniang Bayan Members,
adopt measures to collect from delinquent taxpayers and strengthen
and intensify collection system and tax campaign to minimize tax
delinquencies.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant:
- to record purchases of office supplies, accountable forms, drugs and
medicines, medical and dental laboratory supplies, food supplies,
gasoline, oil and lubricant expense and other supplies expense as debit to
appropriate asset account using the perpetual inventory system
regardless of whether they were consumed within the accounting period;
- to maintain the subsidiary ledger cards for each supplies and materials;
and
- to record the issuances base on the Report of Supplies and Materials
Issued (RSMI).
b. Municipal and Bauan General Hospital Supply Officers:
- to maintain stock cards and property cards for supplies;
- to prepare and submit monthly the RSMI to the Municipal Accountant
for the recording of issuances; and
- to prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories
(RPCI) to report the physical count of supplies by type of inventory as of
a given date.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant set-up and maintain
Special Accounts in the General Fund for the monitoring of operating
performance and viability of the Municipality’s economic enterprises and 20%
Development Fund, and to serve as a decision making tool of the agency. Also,
record all transactions pertaining to these economic enterprises to the
corresponding subsidiary ledgers.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct
the Municipal Accountant to continuously coordinate with the Provincial
Accountant to reconcile the balances of the reciprocal accounts Due from/Due to
LGUs by identifying the causes of discrepancies in the corresponding subsidiary
ledgers for adjustment, if warranted. Likewise, effect the immediate settlement
of obligations of each fund for a fair presentation of the cash position of each
fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Engineer and other concerned officials to submit at the beginning of the year the
lists of all on-going government projects/programs/activities (“PPA”) and those
that are to be implemented during the year, in compliance with the above cited
regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to:
• institute all possible legal remedies against the concerned accountable
persons;
• request the write-off of receivables with no certainty of collection, except
for those recorded under the “Receivable-Disallowances/Charges”
account pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005, or follow-up the request
if there’s a pending request for its writing-off from the Commission on
Audit. Submission of all proofs that efforts have been initiated to collect
the same should be undertaken;
We reiterated our recommendation to exert more efforts in locating the
records pertaining to the outstanding receivable balance of the “Study Now, Pay
Later Program” in order to substantiate its existence and make possible the
recovery of the said receivables. Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal
